questions.questionText: HIV is a retrovirus that uses which enzyme to convert its RNA genome to DNA?
questions.optionB: Reverse transcriptase (RNA → DNA, then integrase inserts into host genome)
questions.explanation: HIV: retrovirus (ssRNA, diploid — 2 copies of RNA). Reverse transcriptase (RT): RNA-dependent DNA polymerase → converts ssRNA → dsDNA (proviral DNA). Integrase: inserts proviral DNA into host chromosome → permanent infection. Protease: cleaves polyprotein for viral assembly. Targets: CD4+ T cells (gp120-CD4 binding, gp41 fusion), macrophages, dendritic cells. CD4 decline → AIDS (CD4<200). HAART: NRTIs + NNRTIs + PIs/INSTIs. RT inhibitors: zidovudine (NRTI), efavirenz (NNRTI). Reference: Ananthanarayan — HIV.

questions.questionText: Influenza virus undergoes antigenic shift which causes:
questions.optionB: Pandemics (major change in HA/NA surface antigens due to reassortment of RNA segments between different viral strains)
questions.explanation: Influenza virus: orthomyxovirus, segmented ssRNA (8 segments). Antigenic SHIFT: major change — reassortment of RNA segments between human and animal (avian/swine) strains in mixing vessel (pig) → novel HA/NA subtypes → no pre-existing immunity → PANDEMICS (e.g., H1N1 2009, H2N2 1957, H3N2 1968). Only influenza A (has animal reservoir). Antigenic DRIFT: minor mutations (point mutations in HA/NA) → gradual change → seasonal epidemics (both A and B). Treatment: oseltamivir, zanamivir (neuraminidase inhibitors). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Influenza.

questions.questionText: Rabies virus travels to the brain via:
questions.optionB: Retrograde axonal transport through peripheral nerves (from bite site to CNS)
questions.explanation: Rabies virus (Lyssavirus, Rhabdoviridae): bullet-shaped, ssRNA, enveloped. Transmission: animal bite (dogs — most common worldwide). Travels via retrograde axonal transport through peripheral nerves to CNS (incubation: weeks to months, depending on bite distance from brain). Negri bodies: pathognomonic eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions in hippocampal neurons (Purkinje cells). Hydrophobia, aerophobia. Nearly 100% fatal once symptomatic. Post-exposure prophylaxis: wound wash + rabies vaccine (5 doses) + rabies immunoglobulin (RIG). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Rabies.

questions.questionText: Hepatitis C virus is significant because it:
questions.optionB: Has the highest chronicity rate (~80%), leading to cirrhosis and HCC, and is now curable with DAAs (direct-acting antivirals)
questions.explanation: HCV: Flaviviridae, ssRNA (+sense), enveloped. Highest chronicity (~80% become chronic — compared to 5% HBV adults). Blood-borne (IVDU — most common route, transfusion — now screened, sexual — less common). Chronic HCV → cirrhosis (20-30% over 20 years) → HCC. HCV causes cancer ONLY through cirrhosis (unlike HBV). NO vaccine available (high mutation rate — hypervariable region). Diagnosis: anti-HCV Ab (screening) → HCV RNA PCR (confirmation/viral load). Treatment: DAAs (sofosbuvir + ledipasvir/velpatasvir) → >95% cure rate. Reference: Ananthanarayan — HCV.

questions.questionText: Dengue hemorrhagic fever (DHF) is most commonly associated with:
questions.optionB: Secondary infection with a DIFFERENT dengue serotype (antibody-dependent enhancement — ADE)
questions.explanation: Dengue: Flavivirus, 4 serotypes (DENV 1-4), transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquito. Dengue fever: 'breakbone fever' — high fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash. DHF/DSS: occurs predominantly during SECONDARY infection with a different serotype → antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE): non-neutralizing cross-reactive antibodies from first infection → enhance viral entry into macrophages via Fc receptors → increased viral load → cytokine storm → plasma leakage, hemorrhage, shock. NS1 antigen: early diagnosis. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Dengue.
